behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text explicitly requires an external error-path HTTP contract ("401 response") plus an externally visible non-effect ("body is not forwarded"). I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior requires validating response contract at the service boundary, not only internal function logic.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service with real HTTP ingress. Send a webhook with an invalid `X-Signature-256` over HTTP, assert status is 401, then verify via queue readback/inspection that no event was enqueued.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering guarantee across external response and persistent side effect. The behavior explicitly requires proving side effect visibility before ack (round-trip effect rule / System triad #3). Integration was considered, but rejected because internal mocks can miss real ordering at the service boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Issue a valid webhook over HTTP; at ack time, verify the event is already durably present in the real persistent queue (readback), and that HTTP 200 is returned only after successful write.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Failure mode is cross-module orchestration (handler -> queue/downstream processor) and can be reproduced with in-process wiring and controlled slow processor. No explicit requirement for real OS/network/subprocess behavior beyond module interaction. Rejected System because behavior text does not require real external contract nuances beyond standard response timing relation.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler, queue, and a deliberately blocked downstream processor in-process. Send a valid request through the app stack, assert response is returned before processor completion signal, and event is still scheduled for async processing.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time, concurrency, externally observed latency SLO at HTTP boundary. Real wall-clock and concurrent load behavior can be masked by in-process fakes; explicit response-time contract under contention triggers System.
behaviors.test_description: Start the service and generate concurrent traffic where earlier events induce slow downstream work. Measure end-to-end HTTP latency for new requests and assert responses remain <= 3s wall-clock under the specified concurrent slow-processing scenario.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: This is dedup/state-propagation across handler + persistence layers (cross-module handoff), not single-function logic. It does not require real network/OS/subprocess if tested with real local persistence wiring. Rejected System because behavior text does not explicitly require external HTTP contract verification or real infra-only failure mode.
behaviors.test_description: With real dedup storage and queue modules wired together, submit the same event ID twice and assert only one durable queue write exists (second attempt no-op/reject).

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly requires external error response contract shape: status code plus specific header (`Retry-After`) under backpressure condition. Integration was considered, but rejected because this is boundary-level contract verification.
behaviors.test_description: Force real queue-full condition, send webhook over HTTP, assert response is 503 and `Retry-After` header is present and correctly formatted/value-constrained per contract.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This targets queue-component behavior with real persistence implementation and configuration interaction (multi-module local infra). It does not explicitly require real external API/network/subprocess boundary checks. Rejected System because behavior text is about queue enforcement semantics, not external HTTP/OS contract verification.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue implementation with a small capacity, enqueue up to limit, then enqueue one extra event and assert explicit rejection and unchanged persisted depth.
